~State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
File 
Date 
Nov. 16, 1976 
From 
fAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Ken Finney 
Subject: 
In a telephone conversation today with Mr. Robert D. Blasier, 
a Deputy District Attorney in Contra Costa County, I discussed 
his letter of October 26, 1976, and the issues raised therein. 
I indicated to Mr. Blasier that the facts contained in his let-
ter do demonstrate that Dr. Wheeler does have a conflict of 
interest and would be prohibited by Section 87100 from making, 
participating or using his official position to influence a 
decision relative to whether the land which is used by his 
hunting club should be flooded. 
This is because Dr. Wheeler 
concedes that he has a financial interest within the meaning 
of the Political Reform Act and it also is readily apparent 
that it is foreseeable that a decision not to flood the land 
would have a material financial effect on that interest, since 
it would mean that the land would not be usable for hunting pur-
poses and Dr. Wheeler would not be able to sell memberships in 
his club. 
The difficult issue, however, is to what extent Dr. Wheeler 
can participate in decisions about whether to flood the land 
as a member of the public. 
I indicated to Mr. Blasier that 
Dr. Wheeler could not ask that the land be flooded in his 
capacity as a board member, nor could he participate in dis-
cussions about whether to flood in his capacity as a board 
member. 
I pointed out, however, that he probably does have 
some right, pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700, to 
represent himself before the board because this is a matter 
related to his personal interests. 
In that connection, I 
pointed out that the Commission has not decided what the 
limits of that participation are but has indicated that some 
participation, even before a member's own board or commission, 
is permissible. 
This probably means that Dr. Wheeler could 
at least participate as a member of the public, assuming that 
he is subject to the same restrictions as any other member of 
the public when discussing a flooding decision. 
Finally, I indicated to Mr. Blasier that I would confirm the 
substance of our conversation in a letter to him. 
KF: jo 


October 26, 1976~ 
. ""fi~q 
Robert stern, Esq. 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Re: 
Possible Conflict of Interest 
Dear Mr. Stern: 
Our office has received a complaint of a possible conflict of 
interest involving a member of the Board of Trustees of the Contra 
Costa County Mosquito Abatement District. 
Based on our understanding of the facts and considering both the 
Political Reform Act of 1974 and the Administrative Code §18700, it 
is the opinion of this office that a conflict of interest does exist. 
I have discussed this matter informally with the attorney representing 
the Mosquito Abatement District, Frederick G. Harris of 425 California 
Street, San Francisco, California 94104, (415) 433-1650. 
I do not 
believe there is any real dispute as to the facts; however, Mr. Harris 
has reached a different conclusion than we have concerning whether a 
conflict of interest exists. 
I have told Mr. Harris that I am more than willing to abide by 
the opinion of your office in this matter. 
Naturally, a formal opinion 
would be the most desireable; however, I will also abide by an in-
formal opinion, so long as it is based on a correct understanding of 
the facts involved. 
The following is a statement of the factual situation. 
I am 
sending Mr. HarriE; a copy of this letter and I would hope that he 
would respond if there is any question about the facts as I have 
stated them. 
Dr. Ron Wheeler is a member of the Board of Trustees for the 
Contra Costa County Mosquito Abatement District. 
The principle 
responsibility of the Abatement District is to take whatever action 
is necessary to alleviate any problems within the county with mos-
quitos, flies, or other insects. 
Apparently, quite some time ago, 


Robert Stern, Esq. 
October 26, 1976 
Page Two 
the Board was responsible for having dikes constructed in the north-
western area of Contra Costa County north of Concord, California. 
The 
purpose of the dikes was to assist in controlling the insect problem 
in that area of the county. 
As part of their responsibility, the 
District has the authority to raise the flood gates on these dikes, 
thus flooding that section of property along the Suisun Bay just west 
of Port Chicago. 
In addition to Dr. Wheeler's position on the Board of Trustees 
for the Abatement District, he also operates the Avon Flats Duck 
Hunting Club. 
The duck hunting club operates in the fall months of 
the year. 
The club is located in the area that is flooded each year 
by the Abatement District. 
The only reason that the flooding is per-
mitted approximately one or two weeks prior to the duck hunting season 
is to make it possible for Dr. Wheeler's duck hunting club to operate. 
Without the flooding, the area would be of no use for Dr. Wheeler's 
duck hunting club. 
Depending on the conditions, Dr. Wheeler charges 
from $90.00 to $135.00 per membership for the duck hunting season. 
This year, because of the warm weather, the flooding that took place 
was partly responsible for the present mosquito problem in our county. 
The sole purpose for the flooding is to make possible the operation 
of the duck hunting club. 
Information we have received from employees 
of the district indicates that Dr. Wheeler has, on occasion, requested 
that the area that he leases for the duck hunting club be flooded so 
that the club can operate. 
Although Dr. Wheeler does not make a large 
profit from operating the duck hunting club, both Mr. Harris and Dr. 
Wheeler have conceded that he does have a "financial interest" as 
defined in Proposition 9. 
The primary question appears to be 
whether or not his requesting that his duck hunting club be flooded 
by the Abatement District of which he is a board member, constitutes 
a conflict as defined in Government Code §18700. 
Mr. Harris maintains that Administrative Code §18700(d) (2) per-
mits a public official to appear before his own agency as a member of 
the general public for the sole purpose of enhancing his or her per-
sonal interests. 
I see little purpose for having any conflict 
provisions if the term "an agency" is interpreted to include the 
public official's own agency of which he is a member. 
I believe this 
interpretation is supported in the very next section of the Adminis-
trative Code, §18700(e), which states that, ". 
.in any way attempting 
-co use his or her official position to influence a governmental 
decision shall include furthering or attempting to affect in any manner 
any decision: (1) within or before his or her agency"; I would argue 
that requesting that your own agency carry out an action whose only 
conceivable purpose is to enhance your own financial interest con-
stitutes a conflict of interest under the Government Code. 
Furthermore, 
Dr. Wheeler has presented no information indicating this his requests 


Robert Stern, Esq. 
October 26, 1976 
Page Three 
to the district for flooding were made by him exclusively as a 
"private citizen" and not a member of the board. 
As I understand it, Mr. Harris maintains that making a request 
that an agency take certain action does not fall within Government 
Code Section 18700. 
It is our position that a public official re-
questing that his agency take certain action constitutes at least 
"attempting to influence" or "attempting to affect in any manner any 
decision" (especially considering that the agency does take the re-
quested action and the sole purpose for that action is to enhance 
a personal financial interest of the board member making the request) . 
Please let us know if you require any further information. 
We appreciate any cooperation you can extend in helping us resolve 
tnis issue. 
RDBJr:pd 
cc: 
Frederick G. Harris 
Attorney at Law 
425 California Street 
San Francisco, CA 
94104 
Very truly yours, 
Deputy District Attorney

sections.conclusion: Based on the facts, Dr. Wheeler does have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act and would be prohibited by Section 87100 from making, participating, or using his official position to influence a decision relative to whether the land should be flooded. However, he probably does have some limited right to represent himself before the board as a member of the general public, subject to the same restrictions as any other member of the public.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a board member of a mosquito abatement district have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when he requests that the district flood land used for his duck hunting club, and if so, what is the extent to which he can participate in decisions about flooding as a member of the general public?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Dr. Wheeler has a conflict of interest and cannot participate in board decisions about flooding in his capacity as a board member. However, he may be permitted limited participation as a member of the general public, subject to the same restrictions applied to other members of the public.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains incoming request letter (October 26, 1976), internal memorandum (November 16, 1976), and FPPC response letter (November 23, 1976). The formal conclusion and question sections are embedded within the response letter and internal memo rather than labeled with section headers. High confidence in extraction due to clear problem statement and explicit conclusion language.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does a board member of a mosquito abatement district have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when he requests that the district flood land used for his duck hunting club, and if so, what is the extent to which he can participate in decisions about flooding as a member of the general public?

CONCLUSION: Based on the facts, Dr. Wheeler does have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act and would be prohibited by Section 87100 from making, participating, or using his official position to influence a decision relative to whether the land should be flooded. However, he probably does have some limited right to represent himself before the board as a member of the general public, subject to the same restrictions as any other member of the public.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that a board member of a mosquito abatement district who operates a duck hunting club on land controlled by the district has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when requesting that the district flood the area. While prohibited from participating in official board capacity, he may have limited rights to appear as a member of the general public.
